quizQuestion: The Green Revolution (1940s–1970s) dramatically increased food production through new technologies. Who is considered the 'father of the Green Revolution' for developing high-yield wheat varieties?
explanation: Norman Borlaug (Nobel Peace Prize 1970) developed high-yield, disease-resistant wheat varieties at CIMMYT in Mexico that were adopted worldwide. The Green Revolution doubled grain yields in developing countries through high-yield varieties, synthetic fertilizers, and irrigation. It prevented famine for hundreds of millions but increased dependence on chemical inputs and benefited larger farmers.

quizQuestion: What key technology, invented by Tim Berners-Lee in 1991, transformed the internet from a military/academic network into a global information platform?
explanation: Tim Berners-Lee invented the World Wide Web at CERN in 1991, creating hyperlinked documents accessible through browsers. The WWW transformed the internet into a global public resource. By 2000, over 400 million people were online; by 2020, over 4.5 billion. The Web is the AP's primary example of technology accelerating globalization.

quizQuestion: The HIV/AIDS pandemic was first identified in 1981. Which region of the world has been most severely affected by AIDS deaths?
explanation: Sub-Saharan Africa accounts for approximately 70% of global HIV/AIDS cases. By 2020, an estimated 25 million people in the region were living with HIV. In countries like Botswana, Lesotho, and Eswatini, adult HIV prevalence exceeds 20%. The pandemic devastated life expectancy, orphaned millions of children, and overwhelmed health systems before antiretroviral treatment became available.

quizQuestion: The COVID-19 pandemic spread globally in 2020 via which mechanism that would have been impossible in previous centuries?
explanation: SARS-CoV-2 spread from Wuhan, China to 150+ countries within 90 days largely because 4 billion airline passengers per year connect the world. Unlike the 1918 flu (which spread via ship in weeks), COVID-19 spread in days. The pandemic killed 7+ million people (officially) and demonstrated that 21st-century connectivity accelerates epidemiological risks alongside economic benefits.

quizQuestion: What intergovernmental scientific body, established in 1988 by the UN and WMO, assesses scientific evidence on climate change and issues influential assessment reports?
explanation: The Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC), established in 1988, synthesizes thousands of peer-reviewed studies by hundreds of scientists from 195 member countries. Its Assessment Reports are the authoritative scientific consensus on climate change. The IPCC's 2021 report stated it is 'unequivocal' that human influence has warmed the climate.

quizQuestion: The 1997 Kyoto Protocol was the first binding international agreement on reducing greenhouse gas emissions. Why was it considered limited in effectiveness?
correctAnswer: US did not ratify it; developing nations were exempt
acceptableAlternatives: excluded developing countries and US refused to join
explanation: The Kyoto Protocol required developed nations to reduce greenhouse gas emissions but exempted developing countries (including China and India) and was not ratified by the United States (the world's largest emitter at the time). Without the US, the treaty covered only 30% of global emissions. It expired in 2012, succeeded by the Paris Agreement (2015).
distractors: It only covered CO2, not methane

quizQuestion: How many parties signed the 2015 Paris Agreement, pledging nationally determined contributions to reduce greenhouse gas emissions and limit warming to well below 2°C?
explanation: The Paris Agreement (adopted December 12, 2015) achieved near-universal participation — 196 parties committed to nationally determined contributions (NDCs) to reduce emissions. Unlike Kyoto's top-down mandates, Paris used bottom-up national pledges. Critics argued the voluntary nature makes enforcement impossible; supporters praised universal participation as a breakthrough for global governance.

quizQuestion: The World Trade Organization (WTO), founded in 1995, replaced what previous body and enforces rules for international trade among how many member nations?
explanation: The WTO replaced the General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade (GATT) in 1995. With 164 member nations (as of 2020), it enforces trade rules, settles disputes, and works to reduce trade barriers. Critics argue WTO rules favor wealthy nations and multinational corporations; supporters credit it with the post-1995 expansion of global trade.

quizQuestion: The International Monetary Fund (IMF) was founded in 1944 at which conference that created the post-WWII international economic framework?
explanation: The Bretton Woods Conference (July 1944, New Hampshire) created the post-WWII international financial system: the IMF (to stabilize currencies and provide emergency loans), the World Bank (to fund reconstruction), and fixed exchange rates tied to the US dollar (gold standard). The system shaped the post-war economic order until Nixon ended dollar-gold convertibility in 1971.

quizQuestion: What controversial IMF policy, required of borrowing nations, demanded cutting government spending, privatizing state enterprises, and liberalizing trade in exchange for loans?
explanation: IMF structural adjustment programs (SAPs) conditioned loans on market liberalization: cutting social spending, privatizing state industries, deregulating markets, and opening to foreign investment. Critics argued SAPs imposed Western free-market ideology on vulnerable nations, cutting health and education spending and increasing inequality. The debate over SAPs became a flashpoint for anti-globalization movements.

quizQuestion: What 1999 protest in Seattle disrupted a WTO ministerial conference, marking the rise of the modern anti-globalization movement?
explanation: The 'Battle of Seattle' (November–December 1999) saw 50,000+ protesters — labor unions, environmental groups, anarchists, and NGOs — disrupt the WTO ministerial conference. Tear gas and arrests shocked observers. The protests crystallized anti-globalization sentiment: that WTO rules prioritized corporate profits over labor rights, environmental standards, and developing-nation interests.

quizQuestion: Al-Qaeda's September 11, 2001 attacks killed nearly 3,000 people in New York, Virginia, and Pennsylvania. Who led Al-Qaeda and ordered the attacks?
explanation: Osama bin Laden founded Al-Qaeda ('The Base') in Afghanistan in the late 1980s and declared war on the United States, citing US military presence in Saudi Arabia, support for Israel, and sanctions on Iraq. The 9/11 attacks triggered the US 'War on Terror' and invasions of Afghanistan (2001) and Iraq (2003), reshaping global politics for decades.

quizQuestion: The September 11, 2001 attacks represented which type of resistance to globalization, specifically targeting American global power and cultural influence?
explanation: Al-Qaeda's September 11 attacks represent religious fundamentalism as resistance to globalization — one of the AP's key examples for topic 9.7. Bin Laden framed his jihad as resistance to Western cultural imperialism, US military presence in Muslim lands, and secular globalization threatening Islamic society. The Iranian Revolution (1979) is the other key example.

quizQuestion: The United Nations (UN) was founded in 1945 to replace the failed League of Nations. How many permanent member nations on the Security Council hold veto power?
explanation: The UN Security Council has 5 permanent members (P5) — the US, UK, France, Russia, and China — each with veto power over council resolutions. The P5 composition reflects 1945 power realities (the main WWII Allied powers). Critics argue it gives outdated powers outsized influence while excluding populous nations like India, Brazil, and Germany.

quizQuestion: The European Union was formally established by what 1993 treaty, creating a single market with free movement of goods, services, capital, and people?
explanation: The Maastricht Treaty (Treaty on European Union, 1993) transformed the European Community into the European Union with three pillars: the single market, common foreign/security policy, and justice/home affairs. It also set the path for the euro currency (adopted 1999 for transactions, 2002 for coins/notes). The EU is the AP's primary example of regional integration.

quizQuestion: Deng Xiaoping's 1978 economic reforms in China launched what policy, combining communist political control with market-oriented economic policies?
explanation: Deng Xiaoping's 'Reform and Opening Up' (改革开放) policies from 1978 introduced market mechanisms, created Special Economic Zones welcoming foreign investment, allowed private enterprise, and opened China to global trade — while maintaining CCP political monopoly. China's GDP grew at ~10% annually for 30 years, lifting 800 million people out of poverty and making China the world's second-largest economy by 2010.

quizQuestion: By approximately what year had China become the world's second-largest economy, reflecting Deng Xiaoping's reform era growth?
explanation: China surpassed Japan to become the world's second-largest economy in 2010. China's WTO membership (2001) accelerated its integration into global trade. By 2014, China was the world's largest economy by purchasing power parity. The rise of China is the AP's primary example of how globalization has shifted economic power away from the West.

quizQuestion: Muhammad Yunus founded Grameen Bank in Bangladesh in 1983, pioneering what financial innovation for poor entrepreneurs that earned him the Nobel Peace Prize (2006)?
explanation: Microfinance provides small loans (often $50–$500) to impoverished entrepreneurs — especially women — who lack collateral for traditional bank loans. Grameen Bank lent primarily to poor rural Bangladeshi women, with repayment rates exceeding 95%. Microfinance spread worldwide as a development tool, though critics argue high interest rates can trap borrowers in debt cycles.

quizQuestion: The UN Convention on the Elimination of All Forms of Discrimination Against Women (CEDAW) was adopted in what year, becoming the international 'bill of rights' for women?
explanation: CEDAW was adopted by the UN General Assembly on December 18, 1979 and entered into force in 1981. It defines discrimination against women, establishes an agenda for national action to end it, and has been ratified by 189 countries (though with reservations from many). CEDAW is the AP's key example of global women's rights institutionalization.

quizQuestion: What South Korean cultural export phenomenon — including music (BTS, BLACKPINK), film (Parasite), and TV dramas — exemplifies cultural globalization beyond Western dominance?
explanation: The Korean Wave (Hallyu) is the global spread of South Korean popular culture — K-pop music, K-dramas, Korean films, beauty products, and food. BTS's global fandom, Parasite's Oscar (2020), and the Netflix show Squid Game (2021) demonstrated that cultural globalization is no longer one-directional from the West. It's the AP's key non-Western example of cultural globalization.

quizQuestion: What term describes the spread of American consumer culture, fast-food chains, and popular culture globally, exemplified by McDonald's opening in 100+ countries?
explanation: Americanization (or 'McDonaldization,' coined by sociologist George Ritzer) describes the global spread of American consumer culture: fast food (McDonald's has 38,000+ locations globally), Hollywood films, blue jeans, Coca-Cola, and English as a global lingua franca. Critics argue it homogenizes local cultures and represents a new form of cultural imperialism.

quizQuestion: The Syrian Civil War (2011–present) created the largest refugee crisis since World War II. Approximately how many Syrian refugees were displaced internationally?
explanation: Syria's civil war (beginning 2011 amid the Arab Spring) created 5.6 million internationally registered refugees and 6.6 million internally displaced persons — about half of Syria's pre-war population. Most refugees settled in Turkey (3.6 million), Lebanon, Jordan, and Germany. The Syrian refugee crisis strained EU unity, fueled right-wing nationalism, and tested international refugee law.

quizQuestion: The Green Revolution increased food yields but also increased economic inequality in many regions. What was a key social consequence of the Green Revolution's reliance on expensive inputs?
correctAnswer: Favored wealthy farmers over subsistence farmers
acceptableAlternatives: widened gap between large and small farmers
explanation: The Green Revolution's high-yield varieties required expensive seeds, fertilizers, pesticides, and irrigation. Wealthier farmers could afford these inputs and dramatically increased yields; subsistence farmers often could not. The result was rural inequality, displacement of small farmers, and increased dependence on multinational agricultural corporations — negative social consequences alongside the population benefits.
distractors: Eliminated all farming in developing nations
Caused mass migration to cities in every country

quizQuestion: HIV/AIDS was first clinically identified in 1981. What development in the mid-1990s transformed AIDS from a death sentence to a manageable chronic illness for those who could access treatment?
explanation: Highly active antiretroviral therapy (HAART), combining multiple antiretroviral drugs, became widely available in 1996. It reduced HIV viral loads to undetectable levels, preventing AIDS and stopping transmission. Access was initially limited to wealthy nations due to cost ($10,000+ per year); 2001 WTO negotiations over generic drug access (Doha Declaration) expanded treatment to developing nations.

quizQuestion: Climate change is driven primarily by the buildup of what gases in the atmosphere, largely from burning fossil fuels?
explanation: Greenhouse gases — primarily CO2, methane, nitrous oxide, and water vapor — trap heat in the atmosphere. Since the Industrial Revolution, CO2 concentrations have risen from 280 ppm to over 420 ppm. The IPCC has established that human emissions of greenhouse gases are the dominant cause of global warming since the mid-20th century.

quizQuestion: What describes the process by which transnational corporations moved manufacturing from high-wage countries to low-wage countries, exemplified by textile production shifting from Europe to Bangladesh?
explanation: Outsourcing moved manufacturing, customer service, and other functions to lower-cost countries. Bangladesh became the world's second-largest garment exporter; China became the 'world's factory.' Outsourcing created jobs in developing nations but led to deindustrialization and job losses in Western countries — a major driver of anti-globalization sentiment.

quizQuestion: The United Nations was founded in 1945 with how many original member states, replacing the League of Nations?
explanation: The UN was founded by 51 original member states who signed the UN Charter in San Francisco on June 26, 1945. By 2023, UN membership had grown to 193 nations, reflecting decolonization's wave of new states. The UN's founding structure — Security Council permanent membership — reflected 1945 power realities and has been criticized as unrepresentative of today's world.

quizQuestion: What does the term 'globalization' fundamentally describe in the context of the late 20th and early 21st centuries?
correctAnswer: Increasing interconnection of economies, cultures, and politics
explanation: Globalization describes the accelerating interconnection of the world's economies, cultures, and political systems through trade, investment, migration, communication technology, and international institutions. International trade grew from 15% to 25% of global GDP between 1960 and 2000. Globalization has reduced poverty for hundreds of millions while also increasing inequality within countries.

quizQuestion: The 4th wave of feminism (2010s–present) is characterized by what digital mechanism for organizing and publicizing cases of sexual harassment and assault?
explanation: The #MeToo movement, originating as a hashtag in 2017 following Harvey Weinstein's exposure, used social media to share experiences of sexual harassment and assault globally. It led to the downfall of powerful men in entertainment, politics, and business. The movement exemplifies how digital technology enables rapid global mobilization — a form of 21st-century social activism.

quizQuestion: What term describes English's role as the dominant language of international business, diplomacy, science, and air traffic control in the globalized world?
explanation: English has become the world's lingua franca — the common language used between speakers of different native languages. An estimated 1.5 billion people speak English (375 million as native speakers). English dominates international science publishing, aviation, diplomacy, and internet content. Critics argue this gives English-speaking nations cultural and economic advantages.

quizQuestion: India's IT sector boom in cities like Bangalore and Hyderabad exemplifies what globalization pattern where developing nations gain comparative advantage in skilled services?
explanation: India's IT sector — clustered in Bangalore ('Silicon Valley of India'), Hyderabad, and Pune — grew from global outsourcing of software development, customer service, and back-office operations. Enabled by fiber optic cables, internet, and India's English-speaking engineering graduates, Indian IT exports grew from $100 million (1990) to $200 billion (2022), transforming a former agrarian economy.

quizQuestion: What global agreement, made under the UN Framework Convention on Climate Change and signed in Paris in 2015, marked the first universal commitment by nearly all nations to address climate change?
explanation: The Paris Agreement (December 2015) commits 196 parties to limit global temperature increase to well below 2°C above pre-industrial levels, with efforts to limit it to 1.5°C. Each nation submits nationally determined contributions (NDCs) to reduce emissions. Despite universal participation, critics note the voluntary NDCs are insufficient to meet the temperature targets.

quizQuestion: What was the transistor's significance for the digital technology revolution of the late 20th century?
explanation: The transistor (invented at Bell Labs, 1947) replaced vacuum tubes as the basic electronic switching component. Transistors could be miniaturized into integrated circuits (chips). Gordon Moore's 1965 observation ('Moore's Law') that transistor density doubles every two years correctly predicted decades of exponential computing power growth — the foundation of the digital revolution.

quizQuestion: What is 'cultural hybridity' in the context of globalization, exemplified by Bollywood films combining Indian traditions with Hollywood production styles?
correctAnswer: Fusion of local and global cultural elements
acceptableAlternatives: blending of local and global cultures
explanation: Cultural hybridity describes how globalization produces new cultural forms through the blending of local and global influences. Bollywood combines Indian musical and dramatic traditions with Hollywood production values; K-pop blends Korean pop music with American hip-hop influences; Nigerian Afrobeats blends African rhythms with American R&B. Hybridity challenges fears of cultural homogenization.
distractors: Complete replacement of local culture by global culture
Rejection of all foreign cultural influences

quizQuestion: What AP historical thinking skill asks students to examine both continuities — persistent global inequality — and changes — new communication technologies — across the globalization era?
explanation: Continuity and Change Over Time (CCOT) is one of the AP's four historical thinking skills. For unit 9, students must identify what persisted from earlier periods (global inequality, great power competition, religious tensions) alongside genuine changes (internet communication, new institutions, shifts in economic power to Asia). The skill requires explaining WHY some things changed while others continued.

quizQuestion: The Taliban seized control of Afghanistan in 1996 (and again in 2021), imposing strict Islamic law including banning girls from education. This exemplifies what form of anti-globalization resistance?
explanation: The Taliban's rule exemplifies religious fundamentalism as resistance to globalization — the rejection of Western secular values, women's rights, democracy, and cultural modernity in favor of a rigidly interpreted Islamic law. The CED (topic 9.7) pairs the Taliban and Iranian Revolution as examples of how religious movements resist the secular values embedded in globalization.

quizQuestion: What global telecommunications infrastructure development dramatically reduced international communication costs starting in the 1990s, enabling the internet revolution?
explanation: Undersea fiber optic cables — networks of light-carrying glass fibers laid across ocean floors — connect continents with enormous bandwidth at near-zero marginal cost per bit. Over 400 submarine cable systems carry 95% of international data. This infrastructure made possible the outsourcing of services to India, global streaming, and the real-time digital economy.

quizQuestion: What was the World Bank's founding purpose in 1944, and what criticism has it faced from developing nations regarding its structural adjustment programs?
explanation: The World Bank was founded to provide development loans for post-war reconstruction and then economic development in poorer nations. Critics from dependency theory and developing nations argue its structural adjustment programs (requiring privatization, deregulation, and budget cuts) forced market liberalization that harmed poor and vulnerable populations while benefiting multinational corporations.
distractors: Providing military aid to developing nations

quizQuestion: What concept, associated with 1970s theorists like André Gunder Frank, argues that globalization perpetuates poverty in developing nations by keeping them dependent on wealthy nations?
explanation: Dependency theory argues that developing ('periphery') nations remain poor because the global economic system is structured to benefit wealthy ('core') nations. Multinational corporations extract resources and profits; trade rules favor manufactured exports over raw materials; debt forces structural adjustment. Dependency theory challenged modernization theory and shaped anti-globalization movements.

quizQuestion: The 2003 US invasion of Iraq was launched in response to what two claimed justifications that were later found to be false or unproven?
acceptableAlternatives: weapons of mass destruction and terrorism connections
explanation: The Bush administration justified the 2003 Iraq invasion with claims that Saddam Hussein possessed weapons of mass destruction (WMD) and had links to Al-Qaeda. Neither claim was substantiated — no WMDs were found and no operational Al-Qaeda link was proven. The war killed 100,000+ Iraqi civilians, destabilized the Middle East, and led to the rise of ISIS.
distractors: Nuclear weapons test and invasion of Kuwait
Genocide and human rights abuses only

quizQuestion: Refugees are defined by international law as people fleeing persecution. What 1951 UN document defines refugee status and establishes the principle of non-refoulement?
acceptableAlternatives: 1951 Convention Relating to the Status of Refugees
explanation: The 1951 UN Refugee Convention defines a refugee as someone fleeing persecution based on race, religion, nationality, political opinion, or membership in a particular social group. The principle of non-refoulement prohibits returning refugees to places where they face persecution. With 80+ million displaced people globally (2020), the Convention's framework is severely strained.

quizQuestion: What does the term 'global civil society' describe in the context of NGOs, international advocacy groups, and cross-border social movements?
acceptableAlternatives: NGOs and social movements operating internationally
explanation: Global civil society encompasses non-governmental organizations (NGOs), advocacy groups, religious organizations, and social movements operating across national borders. Amnesty International, Greenpeace, Oxfam, and the Red Cross exemplify this. Global civil society represents a new layer of global governance beyond nation-states, advocating for human rights, environment, and development.

quizQuestion: What does the 'Arab Spring' (2010–2012) demonstrate about social media's role in globalization, as protests spread from Tunisia to Egypt, Libya, and Syria?
correctAnswer: Social media enabled rapid spread of protest movements
explanation: Beginning with Mohamed Bouazizi's self-immolation in Tunisia (December 2010), the Arab Spring demonstrations spread rapidly via Facebook, Twitter, and YouTube. Social media allowed protestors to organize, share footage of state violence, and inspire others regionally. It demonstrated how digital globalization creates new possibilities for political mobilization — and how governments can also use it for surveillance and repression.
distractors: Traditional media had no role in the uprisings
Foreign military intervention sparked the protests

quizQuestion: China's accession to the WTO in what year marked its full integration into the global economy and accelerated its rise to manufacturing dominance?
explanation: China joined the WTO on December 11, 2001 after 15 years of negotiations. WTO membership required market opening, intellectual property protections, and reduced tariffs. The result: Chinese exports exploded, foreign investment flooded in, and Chinese manufacturing displaced workers in Western nations. China became 'the world's factory' — a central driver of 21st-century globalization.

quizQuestion: What 19th-century economic concept did the WTO promote, arguing that nations should specialize in goods they produce most efficiently and trade for others?
explanation: Comparative advantage (formulated by David Ricardo, 1817) holds that nations benefit by specializing in goods where they have lower relative production costs and trading for others. The WTO promotes this theory, arguing free trade raises global welfare. Critics counter that comparative advantage ignores dynamic development, labor exploitation, and environmental externalities.

quizQuestion: The Syrian Civil War began during the Arab Spring in 2011. Which president of Syria, whose family had ruled since 1970, fought to retain power causing millions to flee?
explanation: Bashar al-Assad (president since 2000) launched a violent crackdown on Arab Spring protesters in March 2011, triggering civil war. Over 500,000 Syrians were killed and 13 million displaced (5.6 million internationally). With Russian and Iranian military support, Assad retained power. The war created the largest refugee crisis since WWII and contributed to European political upheaval.

quizQuestion: What feminist achievement occurred in waves throughout the 20th century, with Saudi Arabia being the last country to grant it only in 2015?
explanation: Women's suffrage spread globally throughout the 20th century: New Zealand (1893, first), US (1920), France (1944), Japan (1945), China (1949), Switzerland (1971), and Saudi Arabia (2015 for municipal elections). The right to vote represents the most fundamental citizenship right; its achievement across most of the world is one of the 20th century's most significant political changes.

quizQuestion: What persistent feature of the global economy — despite rapid growth — represents the key continuity from pre-industrial times into the globalization era, according to AP topic 9.9?
acceptableAlternatives: gap between rich and poor nations
explanation: Global economic inequality is the AP's key continuity in the globalization era. Despite remarkable poverty reduction (especially in China and East Asia), the gap between wealthy nations and the poorest remains vast. The richest 1% own more wealth than the bottom 90%. Technology, trade, and globalization have increased inequality within many wealthy nations even as global averages improve.

quizQuestion: What was the significance of the COVID-19 mRNA vaccine developed by Pfizer-BioNTech in December 2020 for the history of medicine?
correctAnswer: First mRNA vaccine approved for human use
explanation: The Pfizer-BioNTech COVID-19 vaccine received emergency use authorization in December 2020 — the first mRNA vaccine ever approved for human use. It was developed in under a year through unprecedented global scientific collaboration and Operation Warp Speed funding. The mRNA platform (developed over decades) can be reprogrammed for other diseases, potentially revolutionizing vaccine development.
distractors: First vaccine developed in under 5 years
First vaccine made without animal testing

quizQuestion: The euro currency, adopted by European Union members in 1999 for electronic transactions and 2002 for coins and notes, is used by how many EU member states as of 2023?
explanation: The eurozone comprises 20 EU member states that use the euro as their currency (as of Croatia joining in January 2023). The euro is the world's second most widely held reserve currency after the US dollar. Seven EU members (including Sweden, Poland, and notably Britain before Brexit) have not adopted the euro. The eurozone demonstrates regional economic integration — and its tensions during the 2010 debt crisis.

quizQuestion: What development, specifically the Rwandan Genocide of 1994, revealed about the United Nations' limitations as a global security institution?
correctAnswer: UN failed to prevent genocide despite troops present
acceptableAlternatives: UN peacekeepers were present but ordered not to intervene
explanation: During the Rwandan Genocide (April–July 1994), approximately 800,000 Tutsis and moderate Hutus were killed in 100 days. UN peacekeeping commander General Roméo Dallaire warned of the coming genocide and was denied permission to act. Western nations evacuated their citizens and refused to call it genocide. The failure revealed that Security Council politics prevent action when permanent members are unwilling.
distractors: UN acted too quickly without evidence of genocide
UN had no presence in Rwanda
African Union refused to allow UN intervention

quizQuestion: What transformation describes the shift from industrial (manufacturing) economies to knowledge-based (service) economies in wealthy nations during the late 20th century?
explanation: Deindustrialization describes the decline of manufacturing in formerly industrial nations (US, UK, Germany, France) as production moved to lower-cost countries. Steel mills, textile factories, and automakers closed or moved offshore. Service industries (finance, healthcare, technology, education) replaced manufacturing. Deindustrialization created prosperity for skilled workers but poverty for former industrial workers.

quizQuestion: How did the Green Revolution's transformation of agriculture in India and Pakistan in the 1960s–70s demonstrate both the benefits and limitations of technological solutions to hunger?
correctAnswer: Doubled yields but increased inequality and chemical dependence
acceptableAlternatives: prevented famine but benefited large farmers more
explanation: India's Green Revolution (led by M.S. Swaminathan working with Borlaug's wheat varieties) increased wheat production from 12 million tons (1965) to 23 million tons (1972), preventing predicted famines. However, expensive seeds/fertilizers benefited large landowners most; groundwater depletion from irrigation became severe; monocultures reduced genetic diversity. The AP uses it as a model of both technological success and social complexity.
distractors: Completely eliminated hunger in South Asia
Was rejected by Indian farmers as Western imperialism

quizQuestion: What concept describes the pattern where a country specializes in producing raw materials or agricultural products for export while wealthy nations process them into higher-value manufactured goods?
explanation: Commodity dependency — where developing nations depend on exporting one or two raw materials (oil, copper, coffee, cotton) — is a form of structural inequality in global trade. When commodity prices fall, entire economies collapse. Dependency theorists argue this pattern was established during colonialism and perpetuated by WTO rules that make it difficult for developing nations to industrialize.

quizQuestion: What does the rise of authoritarian governments in Hungary, Turkey, India, and Brazil in the 2010s–2020s represent as a countertrend to globalization's expected spread of democracy?
explanation: Democratic backsliding describes the erosion of democratic institutions in formally democratic countries. Orbán in Hungary, Erdogan in Turkey, Modi in India, and Bolsonaro in Brazil were elected democratically but then weakened judicial independence, press freedom, and civil society. This represents a key continuity/change tension: democracy spread after the Cold War but authoritarianism resurged in the 2010s.

quizQuestion: What was the personal computer's significance for economic globalization when it spread to businesses and homes in the 1980s?
correctAnswer: Democratized access to computing and information
acceptableAlternatives: brought computing from institutions to individuals
explanation: The personal computer (Apple II, 1977; IBM PC, 1981) moved computing from expensive mainframes in large institutions to individual desks. PCs transformed business productivity, enabled desktop publishing, and created new industries. Combined with the internet, PCs gave individuals unprecedented access to global information — accelerating the knowledge economy and cultural globalization.
distractors: Replaced all factory workers with machines
Eliminated the need for international trade
Led directly to the space race

quizQuestion: Environmental activism emerged as a global movement in the late 20th century. What 1987 UN report introduced the concept of 'sustainable development'?
acceptableAlternatives: World Commission on Environment and Development report
explanation: The Brundtland Report ('Our Common Future,' 1987), commissioned by UN Secretary General and chaired by Norwegian PM Gro Harlem Brundtland, defined sustainable development as 'development that meets the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs.' It became the foundational concept for the 1992 Rio Earth Summit and environmental governance frameworks.

quizQuestion: The spread of COVID-19 vaccines illustrated global inequality when wealthy nations pre-purchased doses that developing nations could not access. What initiative was created to provide equitable vaccine access?
explanation: COVAX (COVID-19 Vaccines Global Access) was co-led by GAVI, CEPI, and WHO to provide equitable vaccine access. Despite pledges, wealthy nations hoarded vaccines while developing countries waited. By mid-2021, North America had vaccinated 40% of its population; Africa less than 2%. The COVID vaccine inequality became a defining example of how global crises expose structural inequities.

quizQuestion: The Rohingya refugee crisis (2017–present) from Myanmar exemplifies what issue in international law — when people fleeing persecution are denied entry by neighboring states?
explanation: The Rohingya — a Muslim minority in Buddhist-majority Myanmar — have been denied citizenship since 1982 and face systematic persecution. Myanmar's military campaign in 2017 drove 700,000+ Rohingya to Bangladesh. As stateless people denied citizenship, they have limited rights under international law. The crisis demonstrates ongoing failures of the 1951 Refugee Convention's protection framework.

quizQuestion: What was the role of transnational social movements — from anti-apartheid campaigns to climate activism — in shaping global governance in the late 20th and 21st centuries?
correctAnswer: Pressuring states and international organizations to act
acceptableAlternatives: civil society creating pressure for international action
explanation: Transnational social movements created the political will for global governance change: the anti-apartheid movement's international boycotts pressured South Africa; environmental NGOs shaped the Paris Agreement; the anti-landmine campaign (Nobel Peace Prize 1997) resulted in the Ottawa Treaty; Jubilee 2000 pressured debt relief for developing nations. Civil society has become an essential actor in global governance alongside states and corporations.
distractors: Replacing national governments with global governance
Operating without any influence on policy
Functioning exclusively through the United Nations
Achieving change only through armed resistance
